reviews.author: Duy Le - Retirement Expert
reviews.text: There are only a few cities around the world that I would want to revisit again and again, and Hoi An is one of them. Packed in a small area of ancient town are ancient temples, shops and restaurants, historical sites, unique structures, a river with lantern boats, night markets, bars and clubs, hotels and hostels and tens of thousands of tourists per day. The people of Hoi An are extremely friendly. You will find a good photo spot at almost every corner or alley ways. This is what makes this town unique. You can take picture until you run out of storage space and still there is more to discover. At night is when all the people come out and all the action happen. There are lots to do and lots to see. Make sure to have seafood at the end corner of the night market, it is cheap and delicious.

reviews.author: DTD
reviews.text: If there is one place in Vietnam that I would tell everyone not to miss, it is Hoi An. During the day, the Old Town feels peaceful and timeless. The yellow colonial buildings, narrow lanes, and historic houses create a charming atmosphere that reflects its past as a bustling trading port. Walking around on foot is a pleasure — every corner feels photogenic and full of character. But Hoi An becomes truly magical at night. As the sun sets, thousands of colorful lanterns light up the streets and the Thu Bon River. The reflections on the water create a dreamy, romantic scene that is unforgettable. Taking a small boat ride and releasing a floating lantern on the river is a simple yet meaningful experience — calm, beautiful, and symbolic. The people here are warm and welcoming, which makes the whole experience even more special. Shop owners and locals are friendly, patient, and happy to share their culture. Another highlight is the tailoring. Hoi An is famous for its custom-made clothing. You can have a well-fitted suit or elegant dress tailored within just a few hours to a day, at very reasonable prices considering the craftsmanship and quality. It’s a rare combination of speed, skill, and affordability. Hoi An is more than a destination — it’s an atmosphere. A place where history, culture, beauty, and hospitality come together effortlessly. A true must-visit when traveling to Vietnam.

reviews.author: Expeditionsolo Luke
reviews.text: A must see And do in Hoi An! One of the most beautiful places to visit in Vietnam. Has so much to see. The ancient buildings with lots of character, food, coffee, souvenirs, markets, restaurants and bars on the island area of ancient town. The locals are lovely and welcoming here 🙏 definitely a must do place.

reviews.author: Văn Thịnh Nguyễn
reviews.text: Hoi An is one of the most beautiful and atmospheric places in Vietnam. The ancient town, with its glowing lanterns, yellow walls, and riverside charm, feels like stepping into another world. Whether you visit during the day or at night, every corner has something picturesque and full of character. The food is exceptional, the people are warm, and the mix of Vietnamese, Chinese, and Japanese heritage creates a unique cultural experience. Walking through the old streets, trying local dishes, taking a boat ride on the river — everything feels peaceful and unforgettable. Yes, it can get busy during peak hours, but the beauty and atmosphere more than make up for it. If you’re traveling in Vietnam, Hoi An is an absolute must-visit — a place that stays with you long after you leave.

reviews.author: noel medina
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town is well worth visiting if you can get past the heavy concentration of shops and focus on the history and atmosphere. Take time to visit the old merchant houses and temples, which give real context to how the town developed. In the early evening, the lanterns light up the streets and river, creating a beautiful setting. A short boat ride on the river and releasing a lantern adds to the experience. There are plenty of cafés to stop for coffee, and the town is also popular for custom-made clothing if you have time. Ducking into narrow alleys often leads to quieter restaurants away from the main flow. The area is very walkable and best enjoyed in the early evening, though you do need to stay alert for bikes and motorbikes. Simply walking through the streets, sounds, and lights is an experience in itself.

reviews.author: Colin Humphreys
reviews.text: Very charming. If you want to have any clothes made, this is the place to go. There are hundreds of tailor shops, and they could probably knock up a sir for you in about 6 hours. Prices seemed very reasonable. Also pent of bars and cafes and restaurants to choose from.

reviews.author: M. X
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town is undeniably beautiful, especially in the evening when the lanterns light up the streets and the riverside glows with colour. The atmosphere at night is charming and makes for some lovely photos. That said, it is extremely overcrowded. The main streets are packed with tourists, and prices in these central areas reflect that. Food, souvenirs, and especially tailored clothing and shoes are noticeably more expensive than in many other parts of Vietnam. If you wander just a few streets away from the heaviest tourist zones, you’ll find prices drop significantly and the experience feels more authentic. It’s worth taking the time to explore beyond the main lantern-filled strips. A word of caution on the tailoring: while many shops promote high-quality custom clothing and leather goods, the quality doesn’t always match the sales pitch. Do your research, compare shops, and don’t feel pressured to commit on the spot. Overall, it’s visually stunning in the evening and worth seeing — just be prepared for crowds and tourist pricing.

reviews.author: sushil rana
reviews.text: Hoi An is truly a magical city that feels like stepping into a living postcard. The beautifully preserved Ancient Town, glowing lanterns, and charming yellow buildings create an atmosphere that is both peaceful and romantic. Walking through the streets, especially in the evening, is an unforgettable experience as the city lights up with colors reflecting on the Thu Bon River. The city offers a perfect blend of history, culture, and local life. From traditional temples and the iconic Japanese Covered Bridge to tailor shops and handicraft stores, every corner has a story to tell. The food scene is outstanding—local dishes like Cao Lau and White Rose dumplings are a must-try. What makes Hoi An even more special is its calm and friendly vibe. It’s clean, walkable, and welcoming, making it ideal for solo travelers, couples, and families alike. Whether you’re exploring heritage sites, enjoying riverside cafés, or simply soaking in the slow pace of life, Hoi An leaves a lasting impression. A must-visit destination in Vietnam—rich in culture, beauty, and soul.

reviews.author: Utkersh Bora ubmuse
reviews.text: Hoi An is one of those places that immediately feels special. The old town is beautifully preserved, with yellow buildings, narrow lanes, and lanterns that give the city a calm, nostalgic charm—especially in the evening. Walking through the old town is the best way to experience it. The riverside, small cafés, tailor shops, and local markets create a relaxed atmosphere that’s easy to enjoy. The lantern-lit streets at night are lovely without feeling overdone, and a short boat ride on the river adds to the experience. During the day, it can get quite hot and crowded, particularly in the central areas. Some shops feel tourist-oriented, and prices can be slightly higher than expected, but that’s part of visiting a popular destination.

reviews.author: Ramona Eftimie
reviews.text: Hoi An Old Town is pure magic. Every corner feels like stepping into a story—lanterns glowing softly above the streets, ancient houses shimmering in warm colors, and the river reflecting it all like a painting. The atmosphere is simply enchanting, a blend of history, culture, and gentle charm that steals your heart instantly. Walking through the old town feels splendid in every way: the food, the people, the architecture, the peaceful rhythm of the day and the romantic glow of the night. It’s the kind of place that stays with you long after you leave. Truly unforgettable.

reviews.author: Giang Hoàng
reviews.text: Hoi An is a place truly worth experiencing. This land has endured countless storms and historic floods, yet it has quietly withstood them all and risen again with a remarkable strength and resilience. Day and night, Hoi An is alive with visitors from all over the world. Its most beautiful moment comes when the ancient town lights up, transforming the streets into a warm, glowing, and poetic scene. You can wander along the old streets, enjoy a cup of coffee, and savor the delicious local dishes sold everywhere. For a slower, more meaningful experience, try releasing lanterns and taking a boat ride along the river, admiring the surroundings of this land filled with deep emotions and precious historical values that have been carefully preserved through time. Hoi An is not just a place to visit — it is a place to feel, to remember, and to return to again and again.

reviews.author: ckfoxxer
reviews.text: Hoi An, Ancient Town, the city of lanterns! This is my 2nd time here, and I would probably come back, because I love it SO much!!! The colours, the friendly people, how fast the shop owners can make you something, the food...just everything!! Highly recommend making this city part of your travel itinerary!

reviews.author: Z M
reviews.text: Hoi An is a cool little town that works for families, couples or solo Travelers. Not as crowded during the daytime easier to ride a scooter or a bicycle, and fantastic Vibes at night specially in Ancient Town. Music, drinks and great food around the Riverbanks. The lanterns at night are koo but manly for photos. The river lanterns starts at sunset till 10 pm or so, and m its more of a party vibe 🥳. Everything is amazing at this time of year during the Tet holiday in Vietnam.

reviews.author: Shyamal Baruah
reviews.text: Hoi An at day is so beautiful and serene and by night is pure magic! The food tour was unforgettable! I savored Cao Lau noodles, smoky and rich with tender pork, and a crisp banh mi packed with fresh herbs and tangy flavors, what a taste and damn cheap here. The spring rolls were light and fragrant, and the coconut pancakes melted in my mouth with a hint of sweetness. The night market was alive with vibrant colors as hundreds of lanterns glowed softly, casting warm reflections on the river. The gentle evening breeze carried the aroma of sizzling street food and sweet coconut treats, making every step a sensory delight. Shopping was a joy—beautiful handmade lanterns and we picked up a few lanterns as keepsakes of this enchanting night. After exploring, I enjoyed a strong black coffee and a refreshing mocktail by the river enjoying the beauty of the night. Sitting under the lantern-lit sky, sipping my drinks while watching boats glide past glowing lanterns was serene and magical—a moment of pure peace. The highlight was the lantern boat ride. Floating among hundreds of glowing lanterns on the water felt like stepping into a dream. The reflections danced on the river, creating a picture-perfect memory. Hoi An’s night market and lantern boat ride are a feast for the senses—color, taste, aroma, and tranquility all in one. Never Miss it...

reviews.author: Aydogan Cevikkan
reviews.text: Hoi An is one of those rare places that feels magical from the very first moment — and this view captures it perfectly. As the sun sets, the city slowly transforms into a glowing painting: lanterns reflecting on the river, boats drifting quietly through the water, and warm lights filling every street and corner. Everything feels calm, romantic, and alive at the same time. What makes Hoi An truly special is not only its beauty, but its soul. Walking through the old town feels like stepping into another time, where history, culture, and daily life blend effortlessly. The markets, the river, the small streets, the smiles — everything feels authentic and welcoming. Shopping at the local markets is also part of the experience. Bargaining is absolutely normal here and should be done with a smile. As a general rule, it’s best to start at around one third of the first price and negotiate from there. It’s not about being aggressive, but about enjoying the interaction — most vendors expect it and are happy to play along. The food, the atmosphere, the people, and the rhythm of the city make Hoi An unforgettable. Whether you’re walking along the river at night, floating lanterns on the water, or simply sitting and watching life pass by, the city has a way of slowing you down and making you appreciate every moment. Hoi An is not just a destination — it’s a feeling. A place that stays with you long after you leave, and one you’ll always want to return to.

reviews.author: Darren Robichaud
reviews.text: Beautiful town on the river. The water activity was breathtaking at night. All of the boats were lit up. It was quite awesome to see. The shops had everything from custom shoes and suits to restaurants and cafes. Can't wait to visit again.

reviews.author: Lydia Chu
reviews.text: Hoi An Old Town is absolutely beautiful and full of charm. The lantern-lit streets, yellow buildings, and riverside views make it such a peaceful and picturesque place to walk around. There are lots of small shops, cafés, and restaurants to explore, and the atmosphere at night is magical when the lanterns light up. It can get crowded during the day, but early mornings and evenings are perfect for a quiet stroll. Definitely a must-visit when in Vietnam.

reviews.author: Rohan Wagh
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town is widely considered one of the most atmospheric and photogenic destinations in Vietnam. This UNESCO World Heritage site is famous for its well-preserved yellow colonial buildings, narrow pedestrian streets, and thousands of silk lanterns that illuminate the riverside at night.

reviews.author: Le Hoang Giap
reviews.text: I visited Hoi An Ancient Town on a bright morning and it immediately became one of the most memorable places I’ve explored in Vietnam. The entire old town feels like stepping into a living postcard with narrow lantern‑lined streets, well‑preserved historic buildings, and charming shops that blend seamlessly into the centuries‑old architecture. Walking through the heart of town, you quickly sense how much history is layered into every corner, from traditional merchant houses and wooden temples to faded facades that tell stories of trade, culture, and community over generations. The atmosphere is uniquely peaceful in the early hours before the crowds arrive. I wandered along the riverfront promenades and felt drawn to spots where locals and small vendors quietly go about their day. By mid‑morning the town fills up, but instead of feeling overwhelming, the buzz adds energy that makes the narrow alleys even more fun to explore. Lanterns hang above, cafes open their doors with fresh coffee and pastries, and artisans display handcrafted goods that reflect the town’s rich artistic heritage. One of the highlights is the Japanese Covered Bridge, which stands as both an architectural gem and a reminder of Hoi An’s long history as a trading port. The intricate details and peaceful setting make it worth lingering at, especially when the light softens in the late afternoon. Every turn brings something worth stopping for. Traditional markets buzz with activity, offering everything from fresh produce to local specialties. Quaint cafes offer refreshing drinks and light bites if you want a break, and riverside views give you space to sit and watch life drift by. The blend of historic charm and daily life gives the old town a timeless feel that is easy to fall into. Hoi An Ancient Town isn’t just a destination you see, it’s a place you experience. It rewards slow wandering, curiosity, and moments of quiet observation, and it stays with you long after you leave. If you are in the region, it’s a must‑visit for anyone who wants to soak in authentic cultural atmosphere, picturesque streets, and centuries‑old architecture that still feels alive.

reviews.author: Alicia Brant
reviews.text: We enjoyed visiting Hoi A with friends. There are plenty of restaurants with a wide selection to choose from. There are many shops to find whatever you may need. Lots of silk and cashmere and bamboo clothing. Prices are reasonable and remember to barter.

reviews.author: Melvin Narciso
reviews.text: The town is absolutely beautiful—it feels almost magical with the old buildings and lanterns lining the streets and the river. Just a heads-up on the boat rides: be careful which one you pick. Some will take you further out, away from the charming, intimate parts of town, and onto the larger river using motorized boats. Stick to the central area if you want the best experience!

reviews.author: Nur Syuhada
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town is such a beautiful place, especially at night when all the lanterns light up, super magical! The town is really lively with so many tailor shops and cute coffee spots everywhere. They also have lots of linen dresses, super cute and affordable! Just make sure to negotiate a little before buying. Love the riverside view and the chill vibes. You can walk around, try local food, take nice photos, and just enjoy the atmosphere. Definitely one of my favorite places in Vietnam!

reviews.author: John Gledhill
reviews.text: I fell in love with Hoi An ancient town. A well preserved place of history working hard to hold off the influence of the modern world. Captured in time, this is a beautiful place full of charm and somewhere I would like to return and spend a few days especially to capture the town at night where the daytime vibrancy would be amplified to another level. Get this place on your bucket list, it’s an absolute must.

reviews.author: Moonlight85
reviews.text: The vibrant riverfront of Hội An, an ancient city on the central coast of Vietnam known for its remarkably well-preserved architecture and winding canals. The background features the iconic pastel-colored buildings of Hội An's Ancient Town, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The architecture reflects a fusion of indigenous and foreign influences, particularly Chinese and Japanese. Traditional wooden rowing boats, often adorned with colorful lanterns, are a central feature of the Thu Bồn riverfront. These boats are frequently used for tourist cruises, especially at night when the lanterns are illuminated. Vibrant, colorful lanterns are a signature element of Hội An, decorating both the streets and the boats, creating a magical atmosphere after sunset. Taking a small boat ride on the Thu Bồn River is a favorite pastime for both locals and visitors. Walking through the lantern-lit streets to see historical sites like the Japanese Covered Bridge and various Chinese assembly halls. Many visitors participate in workshops to learn the traditional art of making their own lanterns. The city is famous for its local cuisine, including dishes like Cao lầu, a noodle dish made with water from a specific local well.

reviews.author: Jane Streff
reviews.text: If you don’t mind all of the shops, Hoi An’s old city is a lovely place to explore. We purchased the ticket (120,000 VD) to enter five sites within a three day period. We focused on the old homes and a temple. Worth your time, especially if you have time for a leisurely amble through the old city.

reviews.author: Onbarefeet
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town is a must-visit when in Hoi An. The old town’s streets and buildings have beautifully preserved their original architecture and walking through them almost feels like being transported back to the 15th century. Visited about a week before the festive season, whilst the popular spots were busier, many areas were quieter, making it a wonderful time to explore and enjoy a relaxed stroll through this historic town.

reviews.author: YC Khong
reviews.text: Beautiful town especially at night when all lights and lanterns are litted up. Lots of cafes, souvenir shops, clothing, art and craft, etc. There are museums, Heritage buildings, temples also if one needs to learn more about the history of the people here. Visitors can enjoy boat rides too.

reviews.author: Dr. Matt Jacobs
reviews.text: Hoi An Ancient Town has a way of slowly growing on you. It’s not something that hits all at once — instead, the charm reveals itself as you wander through the streets, notice the details, and settle into the pace of the place. The preserved yellow buildings, narrow lanes, and riverside setting create a very calming atmosphere. It’s lovely during the day, but the town truly changes character in the evening, when lanterns light up the streets and reflect on the river. The vibe becomes warm, relaxed, and almost dreamlike. The town is very walkable, making it easy to explore shops, cafés, tailor stores, and small art galleries at your own pace. While it is clearly tourist-oriented, it doesn’t feel rushed — people linger, stroll, and enjoy the surroundings rather than just ticking off sights. One important thing to note is shopping and pricing. Many shops start with very high quotes. For instance, we were quoted 450,000 VND for an item in town, while a government-supported shop had quoted 1,129,000 VND for a similar product. Interestingly, when we walked out of the town shop, the price was immediately dropped to 200,000 VND. This does raise questions about price versus quality — whether the higher-priced government-supported item is genuinely superior, or whether town items are of much lower quality. Either way, it’s clear that bargaining is expected, and prices can vary drastically. The key is to take your time, compare options, and never feel pressured to buy immediately. Walking away often works better than negotiating aggressively. Cultural spots like old houses, temples, and assembly halls add depth to the visit, while street performances and riverside activities bring the town to life in the evenings. It can get crowded at night, and after rain some streets can be slippery, so comfortable footwear helps. Overall, Hoi An Ancient Town is a place to slow down and experience rather than rush through. Go in with realistic expectations, bargain smartly, and enjoy one of Vietnam’s most atmospheric and memorable towns.

reviews.author: Harvey Mansfield
reviews.text: What a beautiful place. The shops make for a great stroll around the town and it is all very aesthetically pleasing with a range of variety of stores. Prices can be good if you haggle or move just off the main street. Wouldn't need more than a day to have a persue about.

reviews.author: GOONER.
reviews.text: Hoi An’s Old Town hits you in a way that’s hard to explain unless you slow the hell down and actually walk it. Come here as a backpacker, not on some packaged tour, and that makes a huge difference. You don’t rush from point A to point B. You wander. You get lost on purpose. And Hoi An is perfect for that. The old streets are small, slightly crooked, and full of surprises. One minute you’re walking past yellow-walled houses with peeling paint, the next you’re standing in front of a centuries-old temple or a tiny tailor shop run by the same family for generations. What really gets you is the atmosphere. During the day, it’s chill. Locals ride by on bicycles, shop owners sit outside drinking tea, and tourists move at a slower pace than in most Vietnamese cities. No horns blaring nonstop, no chaotic traffic. Just footsteps, quiet conversations, and the sound of the river nearby. It feels like the town collectively agreed to take it easy. Then night falls, and the place shifts gear. Lanterns light up the streets, the Thu Bon River reflects every color imaginable, and yeah—it’s touristy as hell. But honestly? It’s still beautiful. As a backpacker, you learn to separate “touristy” from “worth seeing,” and Hoi An sits comfortably in the second category. Grab a cheap beer, sit by the river, watch lanterns float by, and you’ll get it. Food is another win. You don’t need fancy restaurants here. Street food and small local spots are where it’s at. Cao lầu, bánh mì, white rose dumplings—you can eat like a king on a backpacker budget. Some places are clearly optimized for tourists, but if you walk a bit farther from the main streets, prices drop and flavors get way more legit. What I liked most is how walkable everything is. You don’t need a motorbike, you don’t need a plan. Just wake up, step outside, and let the town guide you. It’s easy to spend hours doing absolutely nothing productive—sitting in a café, people-watching, or taking photos of the same yellow wall from ten different angles. Is Hoi An perfect? Nah. At peak hours, it gets crowded, and some parts feel like they’re selling nostalgia on repeat. But even with that, it doesn’t feel soulless. There’s still something real underneath the lanterns and souvenir shops. For backpackers, Hoi An isn’t about ticking off attractions. It’s about slowing down, blending in a bit, and enjoying a town that knows exactly who it is—and doesn’t need to shout about it. 🇻🇳🇻🇳🇻🇳
